answer: An inexperienced applicator let the moxa burn close enough to turn Sugi’s skin reddish-pink before removing it, despite her repeated requests that it be taken off.
evidence:
“It’s okay, I’ll put it out right away with a snap. This is just an accessory, too.”
In fact, I had never applied moxibustion before either, but relying on hearsay and trial and error, I painstakingly attached a large moxa cone, hardened with glue, to Sugi-san’s butt so it wouldn’t fall off, and lit it with an incense stick.
The acrid smell of burning moxa stung my nose. Even as I set the camera position and directed her poses, the moxa burned rapidly. “Ah, ah, it’s hot, take it off, take it off…”
Before two minutes had even passed, the flames were already closing in on her skin. In a panic, I grabbed the burning moxa and tossed it into the ashtray, but it was no wonder Sugi-san was squirming. That part of her skin had already turned a reddish-pink.
“I’ve learned my lesson. This kind of thing…”
expected_question: According to the translated 1955 essay “The Quality of Bondage Models,” which magazine’s later nature and direction were shaped by Tanako Kawabata’s bondage work?
paraphrase_rationale: The replacement trades a bare magazine-name lookup for a concrete, critically framed example of how minimizing a painful effect led to preventable harm.
question: What safety failure does the translated 1955 essay reveal after moxibustion was dismissed as a mere photographic “accessory”?
reason: The replacement trades a bare magazine-name lookup for a concrete, critically framed example of how minimizing a painful effect led to preventable harm.
